Productivity

Programming

Adhd

Design

Product Management

A simple framework to stop the overwhelm and start riding your own momentum.

3 min read
Just now

--

Standard time management advice often feels like an insult if you have ADHD. We are told to plan weeks ahead or color-code every detail. But our brains work on a binary clock. Things are either happening “Now” or “Not Now.” Traditional planning systems do not just fail. They cause you to freeze. The secret to getting things done is not long-term planning. It is managing the relationship between urgency, dopamine, and momentum.

The three pillars: Now, Next, and Later.
Urgency is your ignition switch
Most people rely on long-term goals to stay motivated. The ADHD brain does not work that way. We need urgency, novelty, and challenge to get moving.
When a deadline is immediate, your brain releases the dopamine needed to start. Without that spark, tasks stay trapped in the “Not Now” pile. Because we need urgency to move, a long to-do list feels like a wall of fires. It leads to emotional overload and paralysis. You end up staring at a screen for hours, knowing what you need to do but being unable to start.

The “Now, Next, Later” framework
This system filters the noise and gives your brain the bite-sized urgency it craves. It categorizes tasks by how close you are to doing them.

• Now (The Execution Zone): This holds exactly one task. Not a project, but a single step. Shrinking your focus to one item lowers the barrier to entry.

• Next (The Buffer Zone): This is your runway. It holds the one or two tasks you will do next. This stops the drift where you finish one thing and lose forty minutes down a rabbit hole.

• Later (The Horizon Zone): This is your parking lot. Every project, email, or chore goes here. It keeps your field of vision clear and protects your brain from a massive list.

The power of momentum
The hardest part of any task is getting started. Once you clear that hurdle, you get a spike of dopamine. If you move right to the item in your “Next” slot, you can ride that wave of reward. Your momentum carries you into the next task. Execution becomes easier. You stop relying on panic and start following your own natural flow.

The 15-minute buffer
There is one catch. You cannot jump from a complex project to a new task without a break. ADHD brains have high cognitive inertia. When you are deep in a task, your thoughts leave behind a “residue.”
If you force a switch, your gears will grind. You will lose focus and break your momentum. To stay efficient, add a 15-minute buffer between your “Now” and your “Next.”
During this break, you cannot do “work.” Do not check Slack or read the news. Instead, walk around, grab water, or stare out a window. Let your mind empty its cache. Write down any lingering thoughts from the previous task. This cleanses your palate and gets your brain ready for the next move.

Organization is not about forcing your brain to act like a machine. It is about building a system that respects your pace. By using “Now, Next, Later” and taking short breaks, you stop the overwhelm. You stop trying to manage time and start managing your momentum.
